title: जानकी स्तुति
devanagariText:
॥ श्री जानकी स्तुति ॥

जानकीं रामचन्द्रस्य महिषीं सर्वमङ्गलाम्।
वन्दे कमलपत्राक्षीं सर्वदेवनमस्कृताम्॥ १॥

सीते रामप्रिये देवि विदेहतनयेऽनघे।
सर्वाभीष्टप्रदे मातः जानकि प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ २॥

भूमिपुत्रि विशालाक्षि सुकेशि सुमुखि प्रिये।
रामसेवापरे नित्यं मम सौभाग्यदायिनि॥ ३॥

मिथिलापुरवासिन्यै अयोध्यापतिवल्लभे।
वनवासिनि दीनार्ताशरण्ये नमो नमः॥ ४॥

त्रिजटासमदुःखिन्यै अशोकवनवासिनि।
रावणान्तकरामस्य पत्नि सीते नमोऽस्तु ते॥ ५॥

लवमातरि कुशमातरि वाल्मीकिआश्रमवासिनि।
भूमिप्रविष्टे देवि त्वं जानकि प्रणतोऽस्म्यहम्॥ ६॥

जानकीस्तुतिमेतां यः पठेन्नियतमानसः।
सर्वाभीष्टमवाप्नोति रामसीताप्रसादतः॥ ७॥

॥ इति श्री जानकी स्तुतिः सम्पूर्णा ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Janaki Stuti ||

Janakim Ramachandrasya Mahishim Sarvamangalam.
Vande Kamala-patraakshim Sarva-deva-namaskritam. || 1 ||

Site Ramapriye Devi Videha-tanaye Anaghe.
Sarvabhishta-prade Matah Janaki Pranamaamyaham. || 2 ||

Bhumi-putri Vishalakshi Sukeshi Sumukhi Priye.
Rama-seva-pare Nityam Mama Saubhagya-dayini. || 3 ||

Mithila-pura-vasinyai Ayodhya-pati-vallabhe.
Vana-vasini Dinarta-sharanye Namo Namah. || 4 ||

Trijata-sama-duhkhinyai Ashoka-vana-vasini.
Ravanantakara-Ramasya Patni Site Namostu Te. || 5 ||

Lava-matari Kusha-matari Valmiki-ashrama-vasini.
Bhumi-pravishte Devi Tvam Janaki Pranato Asmyaham. || 6 ||

Janaki-stutim-etam Yah Pathen-niyata-manasah.
Sarvabhishtam-avaapnoti Rama-Sita-prasadatah. || 7 ||

|| Iti Shri Janaki Stutih Sampoorna ||
hindiMeaning: जानकी स्तुति में माता सीता को जानकी, रामप्रिया, विदेहतनया, भूमिपुत्री, मिथिलापुरवासिनी, अयोध्यापतिवल्लभा, वनवासिनी, अशोकवनवासिनी, लव-कुश की माता और वाल्मीकि आश्रमवासिनी के रूप में स्तुति की गई है। उनके जीवन की प्रत्येक अवस्था — मिथिला, अयोध्या, वनवास, लंका और वाल्मीकि आश्रम — का स्मरण है। जो एकाग्रचित्त होकर इस स्तुति का पाठ करता है उसे राम-सीता की कृपा से सर्वाभीष्ट फल प्राप्त होता है।
benefits: Invokes the blessing of all desired blessings through rama-sita's grace
Invokes saubhagya and protection from all difficulties
Especially powerful for women and family welfare
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and leads to spiritual purification
Traditionally associated with divine shelter for those in distress
description: Dedicated to Goddess Sita, the Janaki Stuti (जानकी स्तुति) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. This revered text originates from Valmiki Ramayana. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to bring divine shelter for those in distress as well as grant saubhagya and protection from all difficulties. Additionally, it is known to remove sins and leads to spiritual purification. For best results, devotees chant this on Friday (Shukravar) and during Vivah Panchami and Ram Navami. At 7 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 6 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Janaki Stuti in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Janaki Stuti in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning and transliteration. Beautiful prayer to Goddess Sita (Janaki) for divine grace and marital bliss.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Janaki Stuti and to whom is it dedicated?
seoFaqs.answer: The Janaki Stuti (जानकी स्तुति) is a beautiful seven-verse Sanskrit hymn dedicated to Goddess Sita — the divine consort of Lord Rama and the supreme goddess of the Vaishnava Ramayana tradition. The composition is preserved in classical tradition associated with the Valmiki Ramayana and other Sita-centric Puranic texts. The Stuti opens by invoking Sita as 'Janaki Ramachandrasya mahishim sarva-mangalam' — 'Janaki, the queen-consort of Ramachandra, the source of all auspiciousness' — and progresses through her many sacred names and life-stages: Videha-tanaya (daughter of Videha), Bhumi-putri (daughter of the Earth), Mithila-pura-vasini (resident of Mithila), Ayodhya-pati-vallabha (beloved of Ayodhya's lord), Vana-vasini (forest-dweller), Ashoka-vana-vasini (resident of the Ashoka grove), Lava-Kusha-matari (mother of Lava and Kusha), and Bhumi-pravishta (re-entered into the Earth). (Sources: Valmiki Ramayana; classical Sita stuti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Sita called Janaki, Vaidehi, and Bhumiputri?
seoFaqs.answer: Goddess Sita carries multiple sacred names, each celebrating a different aspect of her divine biography preserved in the Stuti: (1) Sita — meaning 'furrow' — the original name given when King Janaka discovered her as an infant lying in the furrow being ploughed during a yajna for rain in Mithila; (2) Janaki — 'daughter of Janaka' — celebrating her adoptive father, the philosopher-king of Mithila who raised her with supreme love; (3) Vaidehi / Videha-tanaya — 'daughter of Videha' — Videha being the kingdom Janaka ruled, also a play on 'videha' meaning 'bodiless / transcendent'; (4) Mithilesha-kumari — 'princess of Mithila'; (5) Bhumiputri / Bhumija — 'daughter of the Earth (Bhumi-devi)' — celebrating her mystical origin from the Earth itself, to which she ultimately returned through her famous bhumi-pravesha; (6) Rama-priya — 'beloved of Rama'; (7) Lava-Kusha-matari — 'mother of Lava and Kusha', her two divine sons born in Valmiki's ashram. Each name encodes a complete chapter of her divine life. (Sources: Valmiki Ramayana Bala Kanda, Aranya Kanda, Uttara Kanda.)

seoFaqs.question: What stages of Sita's life are commemorated in the Janaki Stuti?
seoFaqs.answer: The seven verses systematically traverse the complete chronology of Sita's divine life: (1) Verse 1 — Janaki as the eternal queen of Rama, the lotus-eyed source of all auspiciousness, worshipped by all devas; (2) Verse 2 — Videha-tanaya, the sinless one (anagha), the granter of all desires (sarvabhishta-prada); (3) Verse 3 — Bhumi-putri (her Earth-origin), the wide-eyed beautiful one always engaged in Rama-seva, the giver of saubhagya; (4) Verse 4 — the Mithila-Ayodhya-vanavasa stages: resident of Mithila, beloved of Ayodhya's lord, then dweller in the forest, refuge of the distressed; (5) Verse 5 — the Lanka period: dwelling in the Ashoka Vatika, suffering equal to Trijata's compassion, wife of Rama who slew Ravana; (6) Verse 6 — the post-Lanka chapter: mother of Lava and Kusha, dweller in Valmiki's ashram, who finally entered the Earth (Bhumi-pravishta); (7) Verse 7 — the phalashruti promising sarvabhishta to the devotee through Rama-Sita's grace. (Sources: Valmiki Ramayana Bala-Aranya-Sundara-Yuddha-Uttara Kandas.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Bhumi-pravesha episode mentioned in verse 6?
seoFaqs.answer: The sixth verse calls Sita 'Bhumi-pravishte Devi' — 'Goddess who entered the Earth' — referencing the supreme final episode of Sita's life as preserved in the Uttara Kanda of the Valmiki Ramayana. After being banished to Valmiki's ashram during her second pregnancy because of public murmurings (loka-apavada), Sita raised her twin sons Lava and Kusha there. Years later, when the boys recited the entire Ramayana before Rama at his Ashvamedha yajna, their identity was revealed. Rama summoned Sita to undergo a second public agni-pariksha (test of purity). Sita, having endured enough, called upon her divine mother Bhumi-devi (the Earth Goddess) — declaring 'If I have ever been pure in thought, word, and deed, may my mother Bhumi receive me back into her embrace.' Immediately the Earth split open, a divine throne arose, Bhumi-devi emerged and seated Sita upon it, and the throne descended back into the Earth — Sita's eternal return to her divine origin. (Sources: Valmiki Ramayana Uttara Kanda.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits are attributed to recitation of the Janaki Stuti?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following phala to sincere Janaki Stuti recitation, with the seventh verse providing the phalashruti 'sarvabhishtam-avapnoti Rama-Sita-prasadatah': (1) all desired blessings (sarvabhishta) through the combined grace of Rama and Sita; (2) saubhagya — sustained marital well-being, harmony, and longevity of spouse — Sita is the supreme pativrata and the principal granter of marital blessings; (3) protection from all difficulties — Sita is invoked as 'dinarta-sharanya' (refuge of the distressed); (4) special grace for women — particularly for unmarried girls seeking a worthy husband, married women seeking marital harmony, and mothers seeking welfare of children; (5) destruction of sins and spiritual purification through the merit of Sita-smarana; (6) family welfare, especially harmony between siblings (recalling Sita's love for Lakshmana), parents-in-law (her devotion to Kausalya and Sumitra), and children; (7) cultivation of pativrata-dharma, patience, and forbearance through meditation on Sita's life; (8) eligibility for Rama-bhakti, since Sita is the path to Rama. (Sources: Janaki Stuti phalashruti verse 7; classical Sita-bhakti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the best time to recite the Janaki Stuti?
seoFaqs.answer: The most auspicious times for Janaki Stuti recitation: (1) Vivah Panchami — Margashirsha Shukla Panchami, the supreme festival celebrating the wedding of Rama and Sita at Janakpur (Mithila), the most auspicious day for Janaki worship in the entire year, especially for marriage and marital harmony; (2) Sita Navami — Vaishakha Shukla Navami, the appearance day (jayanti) of Goddess Sita; (3) Ram Navami — Chaitra Shukla Navami, Rama's appearance day, when Sita is worshipped alongside Rama; (4) every Friday (Shukravar) — the day sacred to Devi worship and marital well-being; (5) every Tuesday for Sita as Bhumi-devi (since Mangal/Mars represents Bhumi); (6) during Navratri — both Chaitra and Sharad Navratri include Sita as a primary form of Devi; (7) before any marital ceremony — wedding, anniversary, conception attempts, child-related vows; (8) for unmarried daughters of marriageable age — the family recites or sponsors recitation; (9) during pilgrimage to Sita-kshetras — Janakpur (Nepal), Sitamarhi (Bihar), Ayodhya, Chitrakut, Hampi-Anegundi (where Sita was abducted), and the ashrams of Valmiki at Bithoor (UP) and Sitamarhi. (Sources: Drikpanchang Sita calendar; classical paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: How is Sita's role significant in the Hindu Ramayana tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: Goddess Sita occupies a supreme theological and devotional position in Hindu tradition: (1) Lakshmi-avatara — Sita is the avatar of Lakshmi (Vishnu's eternal consort) who incarnated alongside Rama (Vishnu's avatar) to share his divine mission; (2) supreme pativrata — the eternal model of marital devotion across all Hindu traditions, invoked at every Hindu wedding ceremony; (3) Adi-Shakti aspect — in the Ramcharitmanas, Tulsidas declares Sita as the Mula-prakriti, the source of all maya and creation; (4) source of Bhakti to Rama — the entire Vaishnava tradition holds that Rama-bhakti is impossible without first taking refuge in Sita, who is karunamayi (the embodiment of compassion) and intercedes on behalf of devotees with Rama; (5) Bhumi-devi connection — Sita is identified with the Earth Goddess herself, making her the source of fertility, prosperity, and maternal welfare; (6) sister of Lakshmi at Janakpur tradition where she is worshipped alongside Urmila, Mandavi and Shrutakirti; (7) inspiration for women's strength — her bearing of forest exile, Lanka captivity, and final banishment makes her the supreme model of dignity and moral strength under suffering. (Sources: Ramcharitmanas Bala Kanda; Adhyatma Ramayana; classical Sita-tattva tradition.)
